source_evidence: Section 4.3, Eq. (12): V_opt = 2M log(tan(pi/4 + phi_F/2)) [1/sigma_T + 1/sigma_C], where phi_F is the latitude of the small rings where the moment is applied. 'The optimal volume is independent of the sphere's radius R.' Obtained by Michell (1904), re-derived by Hemp (1973), Lewinski (2004).
purpose: Provides the analytical closed-form optimal volume of the ball (Michell sphere) structure transferring a moment pair applied on rings at latitude phi_F, used to verify GRAND3 numerical results.
assumptions: Optimal structure to transmit a torsion pair is a ball (curved mutually-orthogonal members on its surface)
Optimal volume is independent of the sphere radius R, but the domain must be large enough to allow the sphere of radius R to develop
phi_F is the latitude of the small rings where the moment is applied
As phi_F -> pi/2 the optimal volume tends to infinity
Michell's formula matches later work provided the quantity L in Michell (1904) is taken as M*r
formula_or_algorithm: V_opt = 2 M log(tan(pi/4 + phi_F/2)) [1/sigma_T + 1/sigma_C]. For a constant-latitude discretization with pi/2 - phi_F = pi/10 and M=7, V_opt = 51.5964.
validation_opportunities: benchmark: GRAND3 spherical-domain result converges to V_opt = 51.5964 (Table 7, Fig. 15)
singularity check as phi_F -> pi/2 (V_opt -> infinity)
convergence test with constant vs increasing phi_F

reuse_risk.explanation: Formula is simple, but results depend critically on how phi_F is held constant under refinement; an increasing phi_F changes the boundary value problem and diverges as phi_F -> pi/2.

missing_information: Ambiguity in Michell's original quantity L (whether L = M*r)
Discretization strategy needed to hold phi_F constant
